1.description: (Color online) Mean WTP in £ £ for Various Inequality Reductions Across Reporting Formats Notes. Mean willingness to pay (with error bands corresponding to the standard errors of the mean) across respondents in each country for each of the four inequality reporting formats (CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio, inequality score, Gini index, two-dimensional format) for each of the three extents of inequality reduction (very high to high, very high to medium, very high to low; see Table 1 , and Table A.6 of Online Appendix A for the coding of inequality levels under the two-dimensional format). WTP was converted from USD to GBP for U.S. respondents using the exchange rate $1 = £ £ 0.75 at the time of the U.S. experiment (November 24, 2020). Sample sizes as in Table 1 .

2.description: (Color online) Distribution of Pay Ratios for Publicly Listed Companies (United States, United Kingdom; FYE2018) Sources. AFL-CIO (2020) and CIPD (2020) . Notes. This figure shows the box plot (1st, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 99th percentiles) together with the estimated kernel density (using the Epanechnikov kernel function) of (i) the distribution of CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio for publicly listed Russell 3,000 U.S. companies in the “Consumer Discretionary” industry in blue (i.e. in the top half of the figure) (for which the pay ratio was collected by AFL-CIO, n = 265); and (ii) the distribution of CEO-to-mean-worker pay ratio for publicly listed UK companies in the FTSE 100 in red (i.e. in the bottom half of the figure) (for which the pay ratio was collected by CIPD, n = 99). The U.S. kernel density was calculated excluding the unique outlier above 4,000 and with a bin width of 100, the UK kernel density was calculated with a bin width of 50. U.S. values above 1,200 are not shown for ease of presentation (see Table A.18 of Online Appendix A for details). For comparison, (red) dashed vertical lines show the four levels of CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio used in our experiments ( Table 1 ). Bold stars indicate the ideal CEO-MW in the ISSP 2009 data ( ISSP Research Group 2017 ; n = 808 for the UK sample, n = 1,378 for the U.S. sample).

3.description: (Color online) Mean WTP in £ £ over Self-Reported Political Leanings (CEO-MW Format) Notes. This figure shows mean willingness to pay (with error bands corresponding to the standard errors of the mean) using the CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio reporting format for each of the three extents of inequality reduction (very high to high, very high to medium, very high to low; Table 1 ) across respondents in each country based on their answer to the question “On economic policy matters, where do you see yourself on the left/right spectrum?” (Table C.1 of Online Appendix C). WTP was converted from USD to GBP for U.S. respondents using the exchange rate $1 = £ £ 0.75 at the time of the U.S. experiment (November 24, 2020). n = 270 for the English sample; n = 540 for the U.S. sample.

4.description: (Color online) Mean WTP in £ £ , Broken Down by Agreement That Country-Level Inequality Is Too Large Notes. These figures show mean willingness to pay (with error bands corresponding to the standard errors of the mean) for each extent of inequality reduction ( Table 1 ) under the CEO-MW format, broken down by country and by subjects’ agreement with the following statement: “Differences in income in the United Kingdom (respectively, United States) are too large.” (a) WTP only among subjects who do not agree with the statement “It is the responsibility of the government to reduce the differences in income between people with high incomes and those with low incomes.” (b) WTP only among subjects who agree with the statement “Most of the time, people with high incomes deserve their high incomes.” Attitudes measured on a five-point Likert scale were converted to a binary “Agree/Disagree” index by mapping Strongly agree and Somewhat agree to “Agree” and the other responses to “Disagree.” WTP was converted from USD to GBP for U.S. respondents using the exchange rate $1 = £ £ 0.75 at the time of the U.S. experiment (November 24, 2020). Of the 83 (respectively, 61) English respondents who disagree with government responsibility (respectively, agree with deservingness), 52 (respectively, 42) agree that inequality is too large. Of the 281 (respectively, 229) U.S. respondents who disagree with government responsibility (respectively, agree with deservingness), 179 (respectively, 158) agree that inequality is too large.
